author: John Chrysostom

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

source.title: NPNF1-14. Saint Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of St. John and the Epistle to the Hebrews

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “In the beginning was the Word.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: On the subject of attention in hearkening it is superfluous to exhort you any more, so quickly have you shown by your actions the effects of my advice.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For your manner of running together, your attentive postures, the thrusting one another in your eagerness to get the inner places, where my voice may more clearly be heard by you, your unwillingness to retire from the press until this spiritual assembly be dissolved, the clapping of hands, the murmurs of applause; in a word, all things of this kind may be considered proofs of the fervor of your souls, and of your desire to hear.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: So that on this point it is superfluous to exhort you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: One thing, however, it is necessary for us to bid and entreat, that you continue to have the same zeal, and manifest it not here only, but that also when you are at home, you converse man with wife, and father with son, concerning these matters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And say somewhat of yourselves, and require somewhat in return from them; and so all contribute to this excellent banquet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For let no one tell me that our children ought not to be occupied with these things; they ought not only to be occupied with them, but to be zealous about them only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And although on account of your infirmity I do not assert this, nor take them away from their worldly learning, just as I do not draw you either from your civil business; yet of these seven days I claim that you dedicate one to the common Lord of us all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For is it not a strange thing that we should bid our domestics slave for us all their time, and ourselves apportion not even a little of our leisure to God; and this too when all our service adds nothing to Him, (for the Godhead is incapable of want,) but turns out to our own advantage?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And yet when you take your children into the theaters, you allege neither their mathematical lessons, nor anything of the kind; but if it be required to gain or collect anything spiritual, you call the matter a waste of time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And how shall you not anger God, if you find leisure and assign a season for everything else, and yet think it a troublesome and unseasonable thing for your children to take in hand what relates to Him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Do not so, brethren, do not so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: It is this very age that most of all needs the hearing these things; for from its tenderness it readily stores up what is said; and what children hear is impressed as a seal on the wax of their minds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Besides, it is then that their life begins to incline to vice or virtue; and if from the very gates and portals one lead them away from iniquity, and guide them by the hand to the best road, he will fix them for the time to come in a sort of habit and nature, and they will not, even if they be willing, easily change for the worse, since this force of custom draws them to the performance of good actions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: So that we shall see them become more worthy of respect than those who have grown old, and they will be more useful in civil matters, displaying in youth the qualities of the aged.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For, as I before said, it cannot be that they who enjoy the hearing of such things as these, and who are in the company of such an Apostle, should depart without receiving some great and remarkable advantage, be it man, woman, or youth, that partakes of this table.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If we train by words the animals which we have, and so tame them, how much more shall we effect this with men by this spiritual teaching, when there is a wide difference between the remedy in each case, and the subject healed as well.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For neither is there so much fierceness in us as in the brutes, since theirs is from nature, ours from choice; nor is the power of the words the same, for the power of the first is that of the human intellect, the power of the second is that of the might and grace of the Spirit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Let then the man who despairs of himself consider the tame animals, and he shall no longer be thus affected; let him come continually to this house of healing, let him hear at all times the laws of the Spirit, and on retiring home let him write down in his mind the things which he has heard; so shall his hopes be good and his confidence great, as he feels his progress by experience.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For when the devil sees the law of God written in the soul, and the heart become tablets to write it on, he will not approach any more.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Since wherever the king’s writing is, not engraved on a pillar of brass, but stamped by the Holy Ghost on a mind loving God, and bright with abundant grace, that (evil one) will not be able even to look at it, but from afar will turn his back upon us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For nothing is so terrible to him and to the thoughts which are suggested by him as a mind careful about Divine matters, and a soul which ever hangs over this fountain.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Such an one can nothing present annoy, even though it be displeasing; nothing puff up or make proud, even though it be favorable; but amidst all this storm and surge it will even enjoy a great calm.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For confusion arises within us, not from the nature of circumstances, but from the infirmity of our minds; for if we were thus affected by reason of what befalls us, then, (as we all sail the same sea, and it is impossible to escape waves and spray,) all men must needs be troubled; but if there are some who stand beyond the influence of the storm and the raging sea, then it is clear that it is not circumstances which make the storm, but the condition of our own mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If therefore we so order the mind that it may bear all things contentedly, we shall have no storm nor even a ripple, but always a clear calm.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: After professing that I should say nothing on these points, I know not how I have been carried away into such a length of exhortation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Pardon my prolixity; for I fear, yes, I greatly fear lest this zeal of ours should ever become weaker.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Did I feel confident respecting it, I would not now have said to you anything on these matters, since it is sufficient to make all things easy to you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But it is time in what follows to proceed to the matters proposed for consideration to-day; that you may not come weary to the contest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For we have contests against the enemies of the truth, against those who use every artifice to destroy the honor of the Son of God, or rather their own.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: This remains for ever as it now is, nothing lessened by the blaspheming tongue, but they, by seeking eagerly to pull down Him whom they say they worship, fill their faces with shame and their souls with punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What then do they say when we assert what we have asserted?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “That the words, ‘in the beginning was the Word,’ do not denote eternity absolutely, for that this same expression was used also concerning heaven and earth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What enormous shamelessness and irreverence!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: I speak to thee concerning God, and dost thou bring the earth into the argument, and men who are of the earth?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: At this rate, since Christ is called Son of God, and God, Man who is called Son of God must be God also.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For, “I have said, Ye are Gods, and all of you are children of the Most High.” ( Ps. lxxxii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Wilt thou contend with the Only-Begotten concerning Sonship, and assert that in that respect He enjoys nothing more than thou?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “By no means,” is the reply.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And yet thou doest this even though thou say not so in words.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Because thou sayest that thou by grace art partaker of the adoption, and He in like manner.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For by saying that He is not Son by nature, thou only makest him to be so by grace.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: However, let us see the proofs which they produce to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “In the beginning,” it is said, “God made the Heaven and the earth, and the earth was invisible and unformed.” ( Gen. i.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And, “There ‘was’ a man of Ramathaim Zophim.” ( 1 Sam. i.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: These are what they think strong arguments, and they are strong; but it is to prove the correctness of the doctrines asserted by us, while they are utterly powerless to establish their blasphemy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For tell me, what has the word “was” in common with the word “made”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What hath God in common with man?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Why dost thou mix what may not be mixed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Why confound things which are distinct, why bring low what is above?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: In that place it is not the expression “was” only which denotes eternity, but that One “was in the beginning.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And that other, “The Word was”; for as the word “being,” when used concerning man, only distinguishes present time, but when concerning God, denotes eternity, so “was,” when used respecting our nature, signifies to us past time, and that too limited, but when respecting God it declares eternity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: It would have been enough then when one had heard the words “earth” and “man,” to imagine nothing more concerning them than what one may fitly think of a nature that came into being, for that which came to be, be it what it may, hath come to be either in time, or the age before time was, but the Son of God is above not only times, but all ages which were before, for He is the Creator and Maker of them, as the Apostle says, “by whom also He made the ages.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Now the Maker necessarily is, before the thing made.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Yet since some are so senseless, as even after this to have higher notions concerning creatures than is their due, by the expression “He made,” and by that other, “there was a man,” he lays hold beforehand of the mind of his hearer, and cuts up all shamelessness by the roots.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For all that has been made, both heaven and earth, has been made in time, and has its beginning in time, and none of them is without beginning, as having been made: so that when you hear that “he made the earth,” and that “there was a man,” you are trifling to no purpose, and weaving a tissue of useless folly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For I can mention even another thing by way of going further.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: It is, that if it had been said of the earth, “In the beginning was the earth,” and of man, “In the beginning was the man,” we must not even then have imagined any greater things concerning them than what we have now determined.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For the terms “earth” and “man” as they are presupposed, whatever may be said concerning them, do not allow the mind to imagine to itself anything greater concerning them than what we know at present.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Just as “the Word,” although but little be said of It, does not allow us to think (respecting It) anything low or poor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Since in proceeding he says of the earth, “The earth was invisible and unformed.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For having said that “He made” it, and having settled its proper limit, he afterwards declares fearlessly what follows, as knowing that there is no one so silly as to suppose that it is without beginning and uncreated, since the word “earth,” and that other “made,” are enough to convince even a very simple person that it is not eternal nor increate, but one of those things created in time.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Besides, the expression “was,” applied to the earth and to man, is not indicative of absolute existence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But in the case of a man (it denotes) his being of a certain place, in that of the earth its being in a certain way.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For he has not said absolutely “the earth was,” and then held his peace, but has taught how it was even after its creation, as that it was “invisible and unformed,” as yet covered by the waters and in confusion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: So in the case of Elkanah he does not merely say that “there was a man,” but adds also whence he was, “of Armathaim Zophim.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But in the case of “the Word,” it is not so.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: I am ashamed to try these cases, one against the other, for if we find fault with those who do so in the case of men, when there is a great difference in the virtue of those who are so tried, though in truth their substance be one; where the difference both of nature and of everything else is so infinite, is it not the extremest madness to raise such questions?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But may He who is blasphemed by them be merciful to us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For it was not we who invented the necessity of such discussions, but they who war against their own salvation laid it on us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What then do I say?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: That this first “was,” applied to “the Word,” is only indicative of His eternal Being, (for “In the beginning,” he saith, “was the Word,”) and that the second “was,” (“and the Word was with God,”) denotes His relative Being.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For since to be eternal and without beginning is most peculiar to God, this he puts first; and then, lest any one hearing that He was “in the beginning,” should assert, that He was “unbegotten” also, he immediately remedies this by saying, before he declares what He was, that He was “with God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And he has prevented any one from supposing, that this “Word” is simply such a one as is either uttered or conceived, by the addition, as I beforesaid, of the article, as well as by this second expression.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For he does not say, was “in God,” but was “with God”: declaring to us His eternity as to person.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Then, as he advances, he has more clearly revealed it, by adding, that this “Word” also “was God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “But yet created,” it may be said.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What then hindered him from saying, that “In the beginning God made the Word”? at least Moses speaking of the earth says, not that “in the beginning was the earth,” but that “He made it,” and then it was.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What now hindered John from saying in like manner, that “In the beginning God made the Word”?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For if Moses feared lest any one should assert that the earth was uncreated, much more ought John to have feared this respecting the Son, if He was indeed created.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: The world being visible, by this very circumstance proclaims its Maker, (“the heavens,” says the Psalmist, “declare the glory of God”— Ps. xix.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 1 ), but the Son is invisible, and is greatly, infinitely, higher than all creation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If now, in the one instance, where we needed neither argument nor teaching to know that the world is created, yet the prophet sets down this fact clearly and before all others; much more should John have declared the same concerning the Son, if He had really been created.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “Yes,” it may be said, “but Peter has asserted this clearly and openly.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Where and when?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “When speaking to the Jews he said, that ‘God hath made Him both Lord and Christ.’” ( Acts ii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Why dost thou not add what follows, “That same Jesus whom ye have crucified”? or dost thou not know that of the words, part relate to His unmixed Nature, part to His Incarnation?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But if this be not the case, and thou wilt absolutely understand all as referring to the Godhead, then thou wilt make the Godhead capable of suffering; but if not capable of suffering, then not created.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For if blood had flowed from that divine and ineffable Nature, and if that Nature, and not the flesh, had been torn and cut by the nails upon the cross, on this supposition your quibbling would have had reason; but if not even the devil himself could utter such a blasphemy, why dost thou feign to be ignorant with ignorance so unpardonable, and such as not the evil spirits themselves could pretend?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Besides the expressions “Lord” and “Christ” belong not to His Essence, but to His dignity; for the one refers to His Power, the other to his having been anointed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What then wouldest thou say con cerning the Son of God? for if he were even, as you assert, created, this argument could not have place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For He was not first created and afterwards God chose Him, nor does He hold a kingdom which could be thrown aside, but one which belongs by nature to His Essence; since, when asked if He were a King, He answers, “To this end was I born.” ( c. xviii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But Peter speaks as concerning one chosen, because his argument wholly refers to the Dispensation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And why dost thou wonder if Peter says this? for Paul, reasoning with the Athenians, calls Him “Man” only, saying, “By that Man whom He hath ordained, whereof He hath given assurance to all men, in that He hath raised Him from the dead.” ( Acts xvii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: He speaks nothing concerning “the form of God” ( Philip. ii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 6 ), nor that He was “equal to Him,” nor that He was the “brightness of His glory.” ( Heb. i.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And with reason.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: The time for words like these was not yet come; but it would have contented him that they should in the meanwhile admit that He was Man, and that He rose again from the dead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Christ Himself acted in the same manner, from whom Paul having learned, used this reserve.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For He did not at once reveal to us His Divinity, but was at first held to be a Prophet and a good man; but afterwards His real nature was shown by His works and words.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: On this account Peter too at first used this method, (for this was the first sermon that he made to the Jews;) and because they were not yet able clearly to understand anything respecting His Godhead, he dwelt on the arguments relating to His Incarnation; that their ears being exercised in these, might open a way to the rest of his teaching.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And if any one will go through all the sermon from the beginning, he will find what I say very observable, for he (Peter) calls Him “Man,” and dwells on the accounts of His Passion, His Resurrection, and His generation according to the flesh.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Paul too when he says, “Who was born of the seed of David according to the flesh” ( Rom. i.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 3 ), only teaches us that the word “made” is taken with a view to His Incarnation, as we allow.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But the son of thunder is now speaking to us concerning His Ineffable and Eternal Existence, and therefore he leaves the word “made” and puts “was”; yet if He were created, this point he needs must most especially have determined.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For if Paul feared that some foolish persons might suppose that He shall be greater than the Father, and have Him who begat Him made subject to Him, (for this is the reason why the Apostle in sending to the Corinthians writes, “But when He saith, All things are put under Him, it is manifest that He is excepted which did put all things under Him,” yet who could possibly imagine that the Father, even in common with all things, will be subject to the Son?) if, I say, he nevertheless feared these foolish imaginations, and says, “He is excepted that did put all things under Him;” much more if the Son of God were indeed created, ought John to have feared lest any one should suppose Him uncreated, and to have taught on this point before any other.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But now, since He was Begotten, with good reason neither John nor any other, whether apostle or prophet, hath asserted that He was created.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Neither had it been so would the Only-Begotten Himself have let it pass unmentioned.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For He who spoke of Himself so humbly from condescension would certainly not have been silent on this matter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And I think it not unreasonable to suppose, that He would be more likely to have the higher Nature, and say nothing of it, than not having it to pass by this omission, and fail to make known that He had it not.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For in the first case there was a good excuse for silence, namely, His desire to teach mankind humility by being silent as to the greatness of His attributes; but in the second case you can find no just excuse for silence.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For why should He who declined many of His real attributes have been, if He were created, silent as to His having been made?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: He who, in order to teach humility, often uttered expressions of lowliness, such as did not properly belong to Him, much more if He had been indeed created, would not have failed to speak of this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Do you not see Him, in order that none may imagine Him not to have been begotten, doing and saying everything to show that He was so, uttering words unworthy both of His dignity and His essence, and descending to the humble character of a Prophet?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For the expression, “As I hear, I judge” ( v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 30 ); and that other, “He hath told Me what I should say, and what I should speak” ( xii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 49 ), and the like, belong merely to a prophet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If now, from His desire to remove this suspicion, He did not disdain to utter words thus lowly, much more if He were created would He have said many like words, that none might suppose Him to be uncreated; as, “Think not that I am begotten of the Father; I am created, not begotten, nor do I share His essence.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But as it is, He does the very contrary, and utters words which compel men, even against their will and desire, to admit the opposite opinion.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: As, “I am in the Father, and the Father in Me” ( xiv.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 11 ); and, “Have I been so long time with you, and yet hast thou not known Me, Philip? he that hath seen Me, hath seen the Father.” ( xiv.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And, “That all men should honor the Son, even as they honor the Father.” ( v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “As the Father raiseth up the dead and quickeneth them, even so the Son quickeneth whom He will.” ( v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “My Father worketh hitherto, and I work.” ( v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “As the Father knoweth Me, even so know I the Father.” ( x.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “I and My Father are One.” ( x.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And everywhere by putting the “as,” and the “so,” and the “being with the Father,” He declares His undeviating likeness to Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: His power in Himself He manifests by these, as well as by many other words; as when He says, “Peace, be still.” ( Mark iv.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “I will, be thou clean.” ( Matt. viii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: “Thou dumb and deaf spirit, I charge thee, come out of him.” ( Mark ix.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And again, “Ye have heard that it was said by them of old time, Thou shalt not kill; but I say unto you, That whosoever is angry with his brother without a cause, shall be in danger.” ( Matt. v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And all the other laws which He gave, and wonders which He worked, are sufficient to show His power, or rather, I should say, a very small part of them is enough to bring over and convince any, except the utterly insensate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But vainglory is a thing powerful to blind even to very evident truths the minds of those ensnared by it, and to persuade them to dispute against what is allowed by others; nay, it instigates some who know and are persuaded of the truth to pretended ignorance and opposition.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: As took place in the case of the Jews, for they did not through ignorance deny the Son of God, but that they might obtain honor from the multitude; “they believed,” says the Evangelist, but were afraid, “lest they should be put out of the synagogue.” ( xii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And so they gave up their salvation to others.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For it cannot be that he who is so zealous a slave to the glory of this present world can obtain the glory which is from God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Wherefore He rebuked them, saying, “How can ye believe, which receive honor of men, and seek not the honor which cometh from God?” ( v.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: This passion is a sort of deep intoxication, and makes him who is subdued by it hard to recover.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And having detached the souls of its captives from heavenly things, it nails them to earth, and lets them not look up to the true light, but persuades them ever to wallow in the mire, giving them masters so powerful, that they have the rule over them without needing to use commands.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For the man who is sick of this disease, does of his own accord, and without bidding, all that he thinks will be agreeable to his masters.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: On their account he clothes himself in rich apparel, and beautifies his face, taking these pains not for himself but for others; and he leads about a train of followers through the market-place, that others may admire him, and all that he does he goes through, merely out of obsequiousness to the rest of the world.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Can any state of mind be more wretched than this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: That others may admire him, he is ever being precipitated to ruin.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Would you learn what a tyrannous sway it exercises?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Why surely, the words of Christ are sufficient to show it all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But yet listen to these further remarks.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If you will ask any of those men who mingle in state affairs and incur great expenses, why they lavish so much gold, and what their so vast expenditure means; you will hear from them, that it is for nothing else but to gratify the people.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If again you ask what the people may be; they will say, that it is a thing full of confusion and turbulent, made up for the most part of folly, tossed blindly to and fro like the waves of the sea, and often composed of varying and adverse opinions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Must not the man who has such a master be more pitiable than any one?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And yet strange though it be, it is not so strange that worldly men should be eager about these things; but that those who say that they have started away from the world should be sick of this same disease, or rather of one more grievous still, this is the strangest thing of all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For with the first the loss extends only to money, but in the last case the danger reaches to the soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For when men alter a right faith for reputation’s sake, and dishonor God that they may be in high repute themselves, tell me, what excess of stupidity and madness must there not be in what they do?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Other passions, even if they are very hurtful, at least bring some pleasure with them, though it be but for a time and fleeting; those who love money, or wine, or women, have, with their hurt, a pleasure, though a brief one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: But those who are taken captives by this passion, live a life continually embittered and stripped of enjoyment, for they do not obtain what they earnestly desire, glory, I mean, from the many.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: They think they enjoy it, but do not really, because the thing they aim at is not glory at all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And therefore their state of mind is not called glory, but a something void of glory, vaingloriousness, so have all the ancients named it, and with good reason; inasmuch as it is quite empty, and contains nothing bright or glorious within it, but as players’ masks seem to be bright and lovely, but are hollow within, (for which cause, though they be more beautiful than natural faces, yet they never draw any to love them,) even so, or rather yet more wretchedly, has the applause of the multitude tricked out for us this passion, dangerous as an antagonist, and cruel as a master.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Its countenance alone is bright, but within it is no more like the mask’s mere emptiness, but crammed with dishonor, and full of savage tyranny.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Whence then, it may be asked, has this passion, so unreasonable, so devoid of pleasure, its birth?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Whence else but from a low, mean soul?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: It cannot be that one who is captivated by love of applause should imagine readily anything great or noble; he needs must be base, mean, dishonorable, little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: He who does nothing for virtue’s sake, but to please men worthy of no consideration, and who ever makes account of their mistaken and erring opinions, how can he be worth anything?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Consider; if any one should ask him, What do you think of the many? he clearly would say, “that they are thoughtless, and not to be regarded.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Then if any one again should ask him, “Would you choose to be like them?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: I do not suppose he could possibly desire to be like them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Must it not then be excessively ridiculous to seek the good opinion of those whom you never would choose to resemble?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Do you say that they are many and a sort of collective body? this is the very reason why you ought most to despise them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If when taken singly they are contemptible, still more will this be the case when they are many; for when they are assembled together, their individual folly is increased by numbers, and becomes greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: So that a man might possibly take a single one of them and set him right, but could not do so with them when together, because then their folly becomes intense, and they are led like sheep, and follow in every direction the opinions of one another.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Tell me, will you seek to obtain this vulgar glory?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Do not, I beg and entreat you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: It turns everything upside down; it is the mother of avarice, of slander, of false witness, of treacheries; it arms and exasperates those who have received no injury against those who have inflicted none.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: He who has fallen into this disease neither knows friendship nor remembers old companionship, and knows not how to respect any one at all; he has cast away from his soul all goodness, and is at war with every one, unstable, without natural affection.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Again, the passion of anger, tyrannical though it be and hard to bear, still is not wont always to disturb, but only when it has persons that excite it; but that of vainglory is ever active, and there is no time, as one may say, when it can cease, since reason neither hinders nor restrains it, but it is always with us not only persuading us to sin, but snatching from our hands anything which we may chance to do aright, or sometimes not allowing us to do right at all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If Paul calls covetousness idolatry, what ought we to name that which is mother, and root, and source of it, I mean, vainglory?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: We cannot possibly find any term such as its wickedness deserves.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Beloved, let us now return to our senses; let us put off this filthy garment, let us rend and cut it off from us, let us at some time or other become free with true freedom, and be sensible of the nobility which has been given to us by God; let us despise vulgar applause.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For nothing is so ridiculous and disgraceful as this passion, nothing so full of shame and dishonor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: One may in many ways see, that to love honor, is dishonor; and that true honor consists in neglecting honor, in making no account of it, but in saying and doing everything according to what seems good to God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: In this way we shall be able to receive a reward from Him who sees exactly all our doings, if we are content to have Him only for a spectator.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What need we other eyes, when He who shall confer the prize is ever beholding our actions?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Is it not a strange thing that, whatever a servant does, he should do to please his master, should seek nothing more than his master’s observation, desire not to attract other eyes (though they be great men who are looking on) to his conduct, but aim at one thing only, that his master may observe him; while we who have a Lord so great, seek other spectators who can nothing profit, but rather hurt us by their observation, and make all our labor vain?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Not so, I beseech you.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Let us call Him to applaud and view our actions from whom we shall receive our rewards.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Let us have nothing to do with human eyes.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For if we should even desire to attain this honor, we shall then attain to it, when we seek that which cometh from God alone.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For, He saith, “Them that honor Me, I will honor.” ( 1 Sam. ii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: And even as we are best supplied with riches when we despise them, and seek only the wealth which cometh from God (“Seek,” he saith, “the kingdom of God, and all these things shall be added to you”— Matt. vi.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: 33 ); so it is in the case of honor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: When the granting either of riches or honor is no longer attended with danger to us, then God gives them freely; and it is then unattended with danger, when they have not the rule or power over us, do not command us as slaves, but belong to us as masters and free men.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: For the reason that He wishes us not to love them is, that we may not be ruled by them; and if we succeed in this respect, He gives us them with great liberality.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: Tell me, what is brighter than Paul, when he says, “We seek not honor of men, neither of you, nor yet of others.” ( 1 Thess. ii.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: What then is richer than him who hath nothing, and yet possesseth all things? for as I said, when we are not mastered by them, then we shall master them, then we shall receive them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom on the Gospel of St. John.
verses.text: If then we desire to obtain honor, let us shun honor, so shall we be enabled after accomplishing the laws of God to obtain both the good things which are here, and those which are promised, by the grace of Christ, with whom, to the Father and the Holy Ghost, be glory for ever and ever.
